title: Sharpening a hand plane iron
subtitle: Hone a plane iron to a working edge on a whetstone in five minutes
excerpt: How to take a dull plane iron back to a working sharp edge on a whetstone. Covers setting the bevel angle, the figure-of-eight motion across the stone, raising and removing the wire edge, and the polishing strop pass.
sourceNotes: Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The blade of a hand plane. A flat steel bar with a bevel ground at 25 degrees and a secondary microbevel honed at 30 degrees for a working edge.

A fine curled flap of steel that forms on the back of the iron as the bevel is honed. The wire edge confirms the bevel has been worked to the very tip of the blade. Removing it cleanly gives the sharp working edge.

content.text: A
plane iron
loses its edge after a half day of bench work on softwood, less on hardwood. The fix is a five-minute pass on a whetstone. The whole job is faster than walking to the ironmonger for a new blade.
When to use this technique

Use this method when a plane stops cutting cleanly: the shavings tear instead of curl, or the surface comes off the wood with visible ridges. The bevel only needs a hone, not a regrind, unless the edge has been chipped. A chipped edge needs a regrind on a bench grinder to remove the damage before honing.
What you need

A combination whetstone (1000 grit on one side, 6000 or 8000 grit on the other) is the standard set-up. A honing guide holds the iron at a consistent angle and is the easiest path for a learner; a freehand pass is faster once muscle memory takes over. A leather strop with polishing compound takes the edge from sharp to mirror-sharp; useful but not essential.
Step 1, soak the stone
Soak a waterstone for five minutes in clean water. Set it on a damp cloth on the bench so it does not slide. The stone is ready when no bubbles rise from the surface.
Step 2, set the bevel angle

Clamp the iron in the honing guide so the bevel sits flat on the stone at 30 degrees. The bevel is the angled face at the cutting end. Most jigs have a setting plate marked for 25 and 30 degrees. Without a jig, rock the iron on the stone until the whole bevel surface meets the stone evenly; the angle is right when the bevel slides flat without rocking.
Step 3, hone on the 1000 grit side

Push the bevel away across the stone, then draw it back. Use the full length of the stone. Make 10 to 15 passes. Move the iron sideways across the stone every few strokes so the stone wears evenly. Check the back of the iron with a fingertip; a fine
wire edge
has formed along the cutting edge when the iron is ready to flip.
Step 4, flip and flatten the back

Lay the iron flat on the stone with the back face down. Hold it firm with both hands. Take six passes back and forth along the stone, keeping the iron flat. This removes the wire edge from the back without rounding the cutting edge.
Step 5, polish on the 6000 grit side
Flip the stone. Repeat the bevel pass on the fine side, 10 passes only. Flip the iron and flatten the back, six passes. The cutting edge is now polished and sharp. Wipe the iron with a clean rag.
Step 6, strop (optional)

Charge the strop with polishing compound. Draw the bevel back across the strop 10 times, then the flat back 10 times. The leather aligns the very tip of the edge and takes the iron to mirror sharp. Skip the strop on a quick hone; add it for a planing job that needs a glass-smooth surface.
What it looks like when it is right

The edge takes a clean shaving across a softwood scrap, curling off in a single ribbon. A fingernail drawn lightly across the edge catches and bites. A pen mark drawn on the bevel is removed by the first stroke on the stone.
Common mistakes
Rocking the iron during the bevel pass. The bevel rounds and the edge angle changes pass to pass. Use a honing guide until the wrist motion is consistent.
Skipping the back flatten. The wire edge stays on the iron and the cutting edge feels sharp but tears the wood. Always flatten the back after each bevel pass.
Honing past a chipped edge without grinding. The hone takes hours to wear through a chip and leaves a wavy edge. Regrind on a slow bench grinder first to remove the chip in seconds.
